response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When I go online, especially on social media, I try to be careful. I don’t spend much time, only a few times a month, so I don’t get caught up in what I see. I remember my parents never trusted the news they heard on radio or TV, and they would always check with others. That taught me to be cautious. I don’t believe everything I see, especially when it’s too dramatic or sounds like it’s made up. For example, I once saw a post about a miracle cure from a small village in the south — it said people were healed overnight. I didn’t believe it because it didn’t match what I’ve seen in real life. I know my faith tells me to trust in God and not in strange stories. I also think about what others say — if many people are sharing the same thing, it might be true, but if it’s only one person, I stay away. I prefer to talk to my neighbors or go to the mosque, where people share honest views. I find peace in knowing what’s real and what’s not. I don’t want to spread lies or be fooled. My life is simple — I bake, walk in the hills, and help at the community center. I trust my own eyes and common sense. That’s how I manage fake content — slowly, with care, and with faith.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is retired, lives in Naples, and has low openness and extraversion but high conscientiousness. With limited social media use, the focus is on personal experience, faith, and trust in reality. The opinion draws from family values, religious beliefs, and practical life habits. The tone is calm, grounded, and cautious, reflecting a person who values truth and simplicity over digital noise.
response_parsed.tone: calm, grounded, cautious, rooted in personal experience and faith
